meta.title: X350/X358 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Jaguar XJ X350/X358 engine – 3.0 V6, 3.5/4.2 V8, supercharged XJR and 2.7 TDVi diesel. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 15 vetted Jaguar specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ X350/X358 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X350/X358 engine typically costs £1,800–£9,000 supply only [JAG-QUOTE], depending on the engine – the 3.0 V6 sits at £1,800–£3,500, while the supercharged 4.2 XJR engine can exceed £9,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,170 at independent specialists. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which X350/X358 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 3.0-litre AJ30 V6 and 4.2-litre AJ33/AJ34 V8 are both solid – they have no major design flaws beyond the cooling system weaknesses (water pump, thermostat housing) that affect all X350 petrol engines. Address those proactively, and either engine will deliver 150,000+ miles of service.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ X350/X358?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system failures on all petrol engines (water pump impeller disintegration, thermostat housing cracks), air suspension compressor failure (Teflon ring wear), and – on the diesel – crankshaft bearing failure on the Ford/PSA 2.7 TDVi unit. Suspension wishbone bushes also wear prematurely.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XJ X350/X358 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It's a well-engineered car but maintenance-dependent. The cooling system is the Achilles heel – address it proactively (upgraded water pump, metal thermostat housing) and the car is reliable. The diesel has a known bottom-end risk that requires careful inspection. The air suspension can be expensive but is well-understood.

mainEntity.name: Which X350/X358 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 2.7 TDVi diesel without documented service history and oil analysis – crankshaft bearing failure is catastrophic and expensive. If you're considering a diesel, have a full inspection (including oil analysis) performed before purchase.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X350/X358 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Coolant loss without visible leaks, overheating, or a water pump failure on petrol engines; knocking from the bottom end on diesel engines; or a persistent "Air Suspension Fault" warning are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XJ X350/X358 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 60% Jaguar scrap threshold. Against a well-kept £10,000 low-mileage 4.2 V8, a £3,000–£5,500 recon is 30–55% – safe. Against a tired £3,000 example, the same recon is 100–183% – walk away. Always factor in the car's condition; exceptional examples may justify the spend.

mainEntity.name: How long do X350/X358 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.2-litre V8 and 3.0 V6 are capable of 150,000+ miles with proper maintenance – the cooling system is the limiting factor. The diesel can achieve 200,000+ if the bottom end is healthy, but the crankshaft bearing risk means many don't reach that figure. Always inspect carefully.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X350, X358 and other XJ generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The X350 (2003–2007) and X358 (2007–2009) are the third-generation XJ, distinguished by their aluminium monocoque body – the first production car to use this technology. The X308 (1997–2003) was the previous steel-bodied generation; the X351 (2010–2019) is the subsequent generation with its own engine line-up. The X350 and X358 share the same engines – the X358 was a facelift with updated styling and electronics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my X350/X358 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block – on AJ-V8 engines, it's located on the left-hand side near the cylinder head. Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it – 15 vetted Jaguar specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Jaguar XJ X350/X358 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X350 and facelifted X358 – from the 3.0-litre V6 and 3.5/4.2-litre V8s to the supercharged 4.2 XJR and the 2.7-litre diesel that brought new buyers to the brand. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the cooling system weaknesses that define this aluminium-bodied generation. Backed by real UK X350/X358 enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar XJ X350/X358 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X350 and X358 – from the entry-level V6 to the supercharged XJR flagship.

overview.intro: The Jaguar XJ X350, launched in 2003, was a landmark car – the first production vehicle to use an aluminium monocoque chassis constructed with aerospace-style rivet-bonding, making it 40% lighter and 50% stiffer than its steel-bodied X308 predecessor. It retained the classic Jaguar silhouette while hiding a host of technological advances beneath the skin, including adaptive air suspension, fibre-optic data transmission and a range of all-new V6, V8 and diesel engines. A 2007 facelift (X358) brought updated styling and electronics.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Jaguar aluminium monocoque (X350/X358) • Predecessor: Jaguar XJ X308 • Successor: Jaguar XJ X351 (2010–2019) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (X350/X358 is the sole XJ body code for this generation)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The X350/X358 generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED], with the 2.7-litre diesel and 4.2-litre V8 accounting for the majority of demand as these cars enter modern classic territory.

items.engine: AJD (2.7 TDVi, 2005–2009) – "The 2.7-litre V6 diesel was a game-changer for the XJ – it brought 40+ mpg economy to the big cat for the first time. Smooth, torquey and reasonably refined, it's the one to choose for high-mileage use. Just budget for crankshaft bearing checks – the Achilles heel of this Ford/PSA unit."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want XJ luxury with diesel economy.

items.engine: AJ33/AJ34 (4.2 naturally aspirated V8) – "The 4.2-litre V8 is the sweet spot of the X350 range – 300 hp, 411 Nm of torque, and none of the supercharger complexity of the XJR. The cooling system is the only real demand – replace the plastic water pump and thermostat housing proactively."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless V8 power without the XJR's maintenance bills.

items.engine: AJD (2.7 TDVi diesel) – "The Ford/PSA diesel can suffer from crankshaft bearing failure – the mainshaft bearings can spin, starving the engine of oil and potentially snapping the crank. This is a catastrophic failure requiring a full engine replacement. Early warning signs include a knocking noise from the bottom end."
items.whoItsFor: only buyers who have confirmed a clean oil analysis and no bottom-end knock.

items.engine: AJ34S (4.2 supercharged V8) – "The supercharger, intercooler and timing chain components combine to make this the most expensive X350 engine to maintain – a full replacement can exceed £9,000. The supercharger coupler can wear, and cooling system failures are common."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want XJR performance and have a dedicated maintenance budget.

items.engine: AJ30 (3.0 V6) – "The entry-level V6 delivers 238 hp, returns reasonable economy and is the cheapest X350 to run and maintain. It lacks the V8's effortless torque, but for wafting around town, it's more than adequate – and it avoids the diesel's crankshaft worries."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want XJ presence on a budget.

items.engine: AJ33 (3.5 V8) – "The 3.5-litre V8 is the forgotten engine of the X350 range – European-market only, it delivers 258 hp and V8 refinement without the 4.2's thirst or the XJR's complexity. Most buyers default to the 4.2, but the 3.5 is a genuine bargain."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 character without the running costs of the larger unit.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid Choice – cheap to run, reliable V6.
Best Value – V8 character, reasonable costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Water pump, thermostat housing, cooling pipes

rows.ownershipVerdict: Expensive – XJR performance comes with XJR bills.
High Risk – catastrophic failure possible; inspect carefully.

economicsRule.title: The X350/X358 rule
economicsRule.text: A £3,000–£5,500 4.2 V8 recon against a £3,000–£8,000 X350 sits at 38–183% of the car's value. On a sub-£4,000 example, that brushes our 60% Jaguar threshold – we'll honestly tell you to walk away unless the car is exceptional. Against a £10,000+ low-mileage example, the same recon drops to a routine 30–55% – a repair decision, not a financial one. Always prioritise cars with documented cooling system work.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X350/X358 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AJ30 (3.0 V6) – cheapest entry, simplest maintenance.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X350/X358 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AJ34S (4.2 SC) – supercharger and cooling system bills add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which X350/X358 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: AJ33 (3.5 V8) – the sweet spot between V8 character and running costs.

problems.title: Cooling System Failures (All Petrol Engines)
problems.affectedModels: X350/X358 V6 and V8 petrol engines (all years).
problems.rootCause: The water pump impeller was originally made from plastic – it disintegrates over time and sends debris through the cooling system. The thermostat housing is also plastic and can crack, releasing coolant. Both can lead to overheating and catastrophic engine failure.
problems.recommendation: On any X350/X358 petrol, replace the water pump and thermostat housing proactively at 60,000–80,000 miles – £400–£800 now is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000+ replacement that follows an overheat. Always fit the upgraded aluminium impeller pump.

cta.label: Compare cooling system repair quotes for your Jaguar X350/X358 →

problems.title: Air Suspension Compressor Failure (All Models)
problems.rootCause: The Teflon ring around the air compressor piston wears thin, preventing a proper seal. The compressor works harder to maintain ride height, eventually failing. Symptoms include an "Air Suspension Fault" message and amber warning light, with diagnostic codes C2302 or C2303.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of an "Air Suspension Fault" warning, have the compressor inspected immediately – a £500–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000 replacement that follows if ignored. Replace the relay at the same time.

cta.label: Compare air suspension repair quotes for your Jaguar X350/X358 →

problems.title: AJD 2.7 TDVi Crankshaft Bearing Failure
problems.rootCause: The Ford/PSA diesel engine can suffer from mainshaft bearing failure – the bearings spin, starving the engine of oil and potentially snapping the crankshaft. This is a catastrophic failure requiring a full engine replacement.
problems.recommendation: If you're considering a diesel X350/X358, have a full oil analysis and crank bearing inspection performed before purchase. Any knocking from the bottom end is a red flag – walk away. Regular oil changes (every 6,000 miles) are essential.

cta.label: Compare AJD engine prices for your Jaguar X350/X358 →

problems.title: Suspension Wishbone Bushes (All Models)
problems.rootCause: The double-wishbone suspension uses rose joints on some bushes, which have a life expectancy of around five years. Symptoms include nervousness over bumps, knocking on rough roads, or MOT failures.
problems.recommendation: Have the suspension bushes inspected at every MOT – a £500–£1,000 repair is cheap insurance against a £1,200–£2,500 replacement. Always get a four-wheel alignment after any suspension work.

cta.label: Compare suspension repair quotes for your Jaguar X350/X358 →
